title: Justice Delayed Becomes Justice Denied!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. The Tamil section is a source-checked reading copy derived from the OCR and does not alter the canonical source file. The malformed duplicate subtitle produced by OCR has been removed, and repairs are confined to unambiguous broken word joins. The source's intentional English maxim, `Justice delayed, is Justice denied`, is preserved as printed. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: All the detailed observations I published on the disproportionate-assets case against Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Jayalalithaa, now being heard by the Bengaluru Special Court, have appeared as a book in both English and Tamil. As a rule, I am not accustomed to criticising courts or the judgments delivered by judges. But it is not I alone; the whole country has watched the extent to which Jayalalithaa’s side respects the courts and the many ways in which it attempts to bend and twist every legal rule to serve its own interests.

As long ago as 18-11-2003, eleven years ago, Supreme Court Justices S.N. Variava and H.K. Sema said in their judgment in the disproportionate-assets case against Jayalalithaa: “It is evident to what extent the prosecution has acted in concert with the accused. It is clear that the public prosecutor has acted in collusion with the accused. This has created in the public mind a credible apprehension that justice will not ordinarily be done. It is certain that justice is being deflected from its course. This case must be taken up and heard every day.”

If the trial is still unfinished despite that stern pronouncement, can we not understand the extent to which the accused have engaged in limitless efforts to delay and prolong it in order to escape, and how far they have succeeded according to plan?

The present Special Judge, John Michael D’Cunha, is acting with concern to conclude the trial quickly and deliver judgment, and with a sense of duty to uphold the law. Yet do you know how many attempts are being made to prevent the judgment from appearing and to postpone it indefinitely?
I have already explained how adjournment after adjournment has been obtained in this case. This letter is written to describe the latest, final-stage efforts now being made to postpone the verdict.

Lex Properties Development Private Limited and other companies were attached in the disproportionate-assets case against Jayalalithaa and identified from the documents as properties of the accused. The case has continued for more than fifteen years. A company that remained silent all those years suddenly approached the Bengaluru court in March, at the crucial moment when the trial was ending and judgment was about to be delivered. It asked to be released from the case and also sought a stay of the main case against Jayalalithaa until its petition was decided.
In other words, its demand was not merely that its property be released. It wanted the trial of the disproportionate-assets case against Jayalalithaa itself to be stayed!
Judge D’Cunha agreed to hear the request to release the companies attached in the disproportionate-assets case, but dismissed the request to stay the main trial.
Lex Properties appealed the Special Court’s decision before the Karnataka High Court. After hearing both sides, Justice Satyanarayana ruled:

“The Special Court at Bengaluru was constituted under the Supreme Court’s directions precisely so that the disproportionate-assets case could be concluded swiftly. The Supreme Court has also ordered that the trial be conducted daily. Even so, numerous petitions are being filed with the object of delaying the case. The petitioner’s request that Lex be released from the disproportionate-assets case is unacceptable, and I therefore dismiss the petition. The Special Court imposed a fine of Rs. 10,000 for wasting the court’s time. As that is too little, I increase it tenfold and impose a fine of Rs. 1 lakh. The amount must be paid within fifteen days.”

That was the judgment delivered by Karnataka High Court Justice Satyanarayana. What makes it still more remarkable is that the same Lex Properties had already once asked to be released from the case, and that request too had been rejected.

Even after the Bengaluru Special Court and the Karnataka High Court rejected its requests, Lex Properties appealed to the Supreme Court. On 8-5-2014, Justices Chauhan, Chelameswar and Iqbal rejected the company’s request for a stay of the disproportionate-assets trial. The Supreme Court judges also censured the company for acting in a manner that obstructed the trial.

In a further attempt to prolong the Bengaluru case, Jayalalithaa’s counsel P. Kumar filed a petition and argued: “Priority must first be given to hearing the petitions seeking the release of the companies attached in the case. On the basis of the judgment delivered by Madras High Court Justice Aruna Jagadeesan on 2 April, the main trial should be suspended and the Bengaluru Special Court should first hear the matter concerning the companies. Only after it is established who owns the companies attached in the case can we prove that the allegations against our client are untrue.”

In his order of 7-5-2014 on the petition seeking the release of the companies, Judge John Michael D’Cunha clarified: “That petition has already been admitted for hearing. The request to stay the main trial until that hearing is completed cannot be accepted. Both matters will proceed simultaneously. A judgment on the petition concerning the companies will be delivered before judgment is given in the main case.”

Going still further, Sivakumar, a director of Lex Properties, filed a petition in the Supreme Court challenging the attachment of the company’s assets and sought a stay of the disproportionate-assets case against Jayalalithaa until that petition was decided. On 13-5-2014, Supreme Court Justices B.S. Chauhan and Sikri refused to grant a stay and adjourned the hearing to 26-5-2014. But on 26-5-2014, the same Supreme Court stayed the disproportionate-assets trial until 6 June and directed the Director General of Tamil Nadu’s anti-corruption wing to file a reply to Jayalalithaa’s petition.

Do we not know what kind of reply the anti-corruption wing, controlled by the Tamil Nadu government, would file? Yet instead of filing any reply by the Supreme Court’s deadline of 6-6-2014, it appeared before the Court that very day and asked for still more time.

Meanwhile, a petition seeking cancellation of the Supreme Court’s interim stay was filed on behalf of our General Secretary, the Professor. Arguing it on 6-6-2014, the movement’s senior advocate Shanmugasundaram said it was wrong to have stayed the disproportionate-assets trial without hearing the government counsel’s argument. Jayalalithaa government’s Directorate of Vigilance and Anti-Corruption, however, pressed for a further four-week adjournment of the trial.

The judges said the hearing could not be postponed for four weeks. They directed Jayalalithaa’s side to reply by Friday the 13th to the petition filed on the Professor’s behalf, and again postponed the disproportionate-assets proceedings in Bengaluru until the 16th. If this brings to mind the proverb, “Even if it sprouts in the Ganges, a bitter gourd does not become a good gourd,” I am not responsible!
Somehow or other, the Supreme Court has postponed the disproportionate-assets trial for another ten days.

In Bengaluru, even after the judge said the accused could present their final arguments, they continued to waste time without doing so. When they again failed to begin their final arguments on 26-5-2014, the judge expressed his condemnation, said the arguments could begin at 3 p.m., and ordered Sasikala and Ilavarasi, accused in the case, to remain in the dock until then.
In its report on the case on 26-5-2014, the English daily *The Times of India* published a long article under the headline, “Assets case may hit Jayalalithaa’s rise to power.”
In an article titled “Judgment Day Draws Near,” the Tamil weekly *India Today* dated 4-6-2014 wrote: “It appears that the judgment may in any event be delivered before the end of this year.”

Meanwhile, in another petition filed before the Bengaluru court on 29-5-2014, Jayalalithaa’s side argued that it had been wrong to attach the properties on the recommendation of the investigating officer, Nallama Naidu. Delivering his order on 4-6-2014, D’Cunha said: “The argument of the accused that Nallama Naidu had no authority to attach the properties is unacceptable. From 1999 until now, the accused have filed more than one hundred petitions. Did it occur to them only seventeen years after the case began that the investigating officer supposedly lacked that authority? It is clear that this is an attempt to delay the case.” He dismissed the petition filed on behalf of the accused and the companies.

I believe all these events provide a good explanation of the many ways in which the disproportionate-assets trial has been delayed. “Justice delayed, is Justice denied,” says the old maxim as printed in the source. Does not justice delayed become justice denied?
With affection,
M.K.
